---
title: "Lab 0: Establish the trusted local baseline"
subtitle: "Recover the SupportOps Inc. workstation, cluster, registry, ingress, and health path"
---

# Establish the trusted local baseline {#sec-lab-00}

SupportOps Inc. inherited a local AI platform repository from a contractor. The
repository expresses part of the intended architecture, but the team cannot yet
prove that the workstation, Kubernetes cluster, registry, ingress path, and
baseline service form a repeatable system.

Your first mission is to establish that trusted baseline. Later laboratories
depend on it for durable data services, training, inference, observability,
security, and GitOps.

::: {.callout-important}
Lab 0 runs from a Linux shell. On Windows, use WSL 2 with Docker Desktop WSL
integration enabled. Keep the repository in the WSL filesystem for predictable
permissions and faster container builds.
:::

## Mission outcomes {#sec-lab-00-outcomes}

After completing this lab, you can:

- validate the workstation contract before changing platform state;
- explain the boundary between Docker, Kind, the local registry, and Kubernetes;
- create the same cluster from one of three resource profiles;
- route a repository-owned image through the local registry into Kind;
- expose and verify the baseline service through ingress-nginx;
- diagnose and recover a controlled availability incident;
- produce automated, text-based evidence for a pull request.

Plan for two to four hours on the first run. Image downloads dominate the time.
Later runs use the local cache and should be much faster.

## Platform state before the mission {#sec-lab-00-before}

The contractor left these useful but incomplete elements:

- a repository-owned technology and curriculum specification;
- three intended workstation resource profiles;
- namespace and health-service requirements;
- a local registry design;
- partial operational expectations.

The platform is not considered trusted until automated verification proves the
complete request path.

## Acceptance criteria {#sec-lab-00-acceptance}

Lab 0 is complete when all of these conditions hold:

| Area | Required evidence |
|---|---|
| Workstation | `make doctor` exits successfully. |
| Cluster | The `supportops-ai` Kind cluster uses the pinned node image and every node is Ready. |
| Profiles | The repository defines 16 GB, 24 GB, and 32 GB topologies. |
| Registry | Docker Registry v2 responds on `localhost:5001` and Kind can pull through it. |
| Namespaces | All five SupportOps ownership namespaces exist with policy labels. |
| Ingress | The pinned ingress-nginx controller is available. |
| Workload | `platform-health` uses the image pushed to the local registry. |
| Request path | `/healthz` returns `ok` through the Host-based ingress route. |
| Recovery | The controlled outage is detected and repository-owned desired state restores service. |
| Verification | `make verify-lab-0` exits successfully after recovery. |
| Operations | The ADR, runbook, and machine-readable evidence have been reviewed. |

: Lab 0 acceptance contract. {#tbl-lab-00-acceptance}

Screenshots can support a pull request, but they do not replace command output,
desired state, or the verification result in @tbl-lab-00-acceptance.

## Architecture review {#sec-lab-00-architecture}

The workstation runs Docker. Kind uses Docker containers as Kubernetes nodes.
The registry is a separate Docker container with its own persistent volume. The
same image name, `localhost:5001/...`, is used from the workstation and from
Kubernetes, while Kind node configuration redirects the node-side request to
the registry container on the Docker network.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-lab-00-architecture
%%| fig-cap: "Lab 0 establishes one verified path from source to the SupportOps health endpoint."

flowchart LR
  Git["Git desired state"] --> Make["Repeatable Make targets"]
  Make --> Docker["Docker engine"]
  Docker --> Registry["Docker Registry v2<br/>localhost:5001"]
  Docker --> Kind["Kind cluster<br/>supportops-ai"]
  Registry --> Nodes["Kind node containerd"]
  Nodes --> Health["platform-health"]
  Client["Local client"] --> Ingress["ingress-nginx<br/>ports 80 and 443"]
  Ingress --> Health
  Verify["make verify-lab-0"] --> Registry
  Verify --> Kind
  Verify --> Client
```

The boundaries in @fig-lab-00-architecture matter during diagnosis. A Docker
failure is not a Kubernetes scheduling failure. A healthy registry does not
prove that a Kind node can resolve it. A ready Pod does not prove that ingress
can route to it.

## Engineering decisions {#sec-lab-00-decisions}

Read `docs/lab-architecture/adr-0001-local-kind-foundation.md` before changing
configuration. The ADR records four durable decisions:

1. Kind is the local Kubernetes foundation.
2. Registry state is independent from cluster state.
3. image and chart versions are pinned for reproducibility;
4. ingress-nginx remains because the curriculum freezes it.

The ingress-nginx project was retired in 2026. This lab pins its final release
and documents the lifecycle risk. Do not substitute another ingress controller
without an explicit curriculum change.

## Step 1: Open the repository in WSL {#sec-lab-00-step-1}

Open a WSL terminal, enter the repository root, and confirm that Git sees the
expected project:

```bash
pwd
git rev-parse --show-toplevel
git status --short --branch
```

The top-level path must be the `ai-platform-engineering-lab` repository. Resolve
unrelated local edits before beginning the student branch.

Create the Lab 0 feature branch:

```bash
git switch -c lab-00/trusted-local-baseline
```

::: {.callout-note title="Tutor checkpoint A"}
Send your tutor the output of `pwd`, `git status --short --branch`, and
`docker context show`. Do not continue until the repository and Docker context
are understood.
:::

## Step 2: Validate prerequisites {#sec-lab-00-step-2}

Run the workstation doctor:

```bash
make doctor
```

The doctor checks Docker, kubectl, Helm, GNU Make, Git, curl, `sha256sum`, Kind,
the Docker engine, and all resource-profile definitions.

If only Kind is missing, install the repository-pinned binary without `sudo`:

```bash
make install-kind
make doctor
```

The installer downloads the correct Linux architecture, verifies the upstream
checksum, and writes the binary under `.local/bin/`. Make automatically adds
that directory to `PATH`.

If Docker is unreachable under WSL, start Docker Desktop and enable integration
for the active WSL distribution. Verify the boundary directly:

```bash
docker version
docker info
```

Do not create the cluster while the doctor reports a failure.

## Step 3: Inspect the reproducibility pins {#sec-lab-00-step-3}

Review the pinned versions:

```bash
cat platform/versions.env
```

The initial baseline uses:

- Kind v0.31.0;
- Kubernetes v1.35.0, pinned by node-image digest;
- Docker Registry v2.8.3, preserving the frozen Registry v2 requirement;
- ingress-nginx chart 4.15.1 and controller v1.15.1;
- BusyBox 1.36.1 only as the base for the static health container.

Tags identify human-readable releases. The Kind node digest makes the node
image immutable. Later security work adds stronger provenance controls to the
broader image lifecycle.

## Step 4: Cache Lab 0 dependencies {#sec-lab-00-step-4}

Cache the node image, registry image, health-service base image, ingress Helm
chart, and every image referenced by the rendered chart:

```bash
make cache-lab-0
```

This step requires Internet access on the first run. After it succeeds, Docker
holds the images and `.cache/helm/` holds the chart. The cache directory is not
committed.

Inspect the result:

```bash
docker image inspect \
  kindest/node:v1.35.0@sha256:452d707d4862f52530247495d180205e029056831160e22870e37e3f6c1ac31f \
  --format '{{.RepoDigests}}'
ls -lh .cache/helm/
```

An offline rerun must use these cached assets rather than silently selecting a
newer release.

## Step 5: Select a resource profile {#sec-lab-00-step-5}

Choose the profile that matches the memory allocated to Docker:

| Profile | Kind topology | Intended workstation |
|---|---|---|
| `16gb` | One control-plane node | Minimum supported configuration |
| `24gb` | One control plane and one worker | Recommended development configuration |
| `32gb` | One control plane and two workers | Expanded local operations configuration |

: Kind resource profiles. {#tbl-lab-00-profiles}

The profiles in @tbl-lab-00-profiles change topology, not platform behavior.
They map host ports 80 and 443 only on the ingress-ready control-plane node.

For the first run, use the minimum profile unless Docker has at least 24 GB:

```bash
export PROFILE=16gb
cat "platform/kind/profiles/${PROFILE}.yaml"
```

Confirm that host ports 80, 443, and 5001 are available. On Linux:

```bash
ss -ltn | grep -E ':(80|443|5001)[[:space:]]' || true
```

No output means no listening process was found. If a port is occupied, identify
and stop the conflicting local service before continuing. Do not change the
platform ports without documenting the architectural consequence.

## Step 6: Create the cluster and registry {#sec-lab-00-step-6}

Create the selected topology:

```bash
make create-cluster PROFILE="${PROFILE}"
```

The target is idempotent. It performs these operations:

1. creates or starts `supportops-registry` with a persistent Docker volume;
2. creates `supportops-ai` from the selected profile and pinned node image;
3. connects the registry to the Kind Docker network;
4. writes a `hosts.toml` registry mapping into every Kind node;
5. publishes the standard `local-registry-hosting` ConfigMap;
6. waits for every Kubernetes node to become Ready.

Inspect the separate layers:

```bash
docker ps --filter name=supportops-registry
curl -fsS http://127.0.0.1:5001/v2/
kind get nodes --name supportops-ai
kubectl --context kind-supportops-ai get nodes -o wide
kubectl --context kind-supportops-ai -n kube-public \
  get configmap local-registry-hosting -o yaml
```

The Registry v2 endpoint returns `{}`. Every node must report `Ready`.

::: {.callout-note title="Tutor checkpoint B"}
Send your tutor the node table, the registry HTTP response, and the
`local-registry-hosting` ConfigMap. Explain why the registry container is not a
Pod inside the cluster.
:::

## Step 7: Install the ingress boundary {#sec-lab-00-step-7}

Install the cached and pinned Helm chart:

```bash
make install-ingress
```

The target loads cached ingress images into Kind before installing the chart.
This preserves the offline-after-cache requirement.

Inspect Helm state and controller placement:

```bash
helm --kube-context kind-supportops-ai -n ingress-nginx list
kubectl --context kind-supportops-ai -n ingress-nginx get pods -o wide
kubectl --context kind-supportops-ai get ingressclass
```

The controller must run on the node labeled `ingress-ready=true`. Its host ports
connect the Kind node’s ports to the workstation ports mapped by the profile.

## Step 8: Build and publish the health image {#sec-lab-00-step-8}

Review the inherited health-service image source:

```bash
find starter-project/platform-health -maxdepth 3 -type f -print
cat starter-project/platform-health/Dockerfile
```

Build and push it:

```bash
make build-platform-health
```

Verify the registry catalog:

```bash
curl -fsS http://127.0.0.1:5001/v2/_catalog
curl -fsS \
  http://127.0.0.1:5001/v2/supportops/platform-health/tags/list
```

The image is intentionally small and serves static health files on port 8080.
It is a foundation probe, not the ML inference service introduced later.

## Step 9: Reconcile the baseline desired state {#sec-lab-00-step-9}

Review the namespace boundaries and workload controls:

```bash
cat platform/foundation/namespaces.yaml
cat platform/foundation/platform-health.yaml
```

Deploy the repository-owned state:

```bash
make deploy-baseline
```

Inspect the result:

```bash
kubectl --context kind-supportops-ai get namespaces --show-labels
kubectl --context kind-supportops-ai -n supportops-platform \
  get deployment,service,ingress,pods -o wide
```

Notice the workload controls: a non-root numeric identity, no service-account
token, dropped Linux capabilities, a read-only root filesystem, health probes,
and explicit CPU and memory bounds.

## Step 10: Test the complete request path {#sec-lab-00-step-10}

Test ingress without modifying `/etc/hosts`:

```bash
curl -i -H 'Host: platform.supportops.local' \
  http://127.0.0.1/healthz
```

Expect HTTP 200 and the body `ok`.

Then test the human-readable endpoint:

```bash
curl -fsS -H 'Host: platform.supportops.local' http://127.0.0.1/
```

If you choose to use the hostname in a browser, add this local-only mapping:

```text
127.0.0.1 platform.supportops.local
```

The Host header is part of the routing contract. A request to `127.0.0.1`
without it can correctly return HTTP 404 from ingress-nginx.

## Step 11: Run automated verification {#sec-lab-00-step-11}

Run the required acceptance command:

```bash
make verify-lab-0
```

The verifier checks cluster existence, node readiness, Registry v2, namespace
ownership boundaries, registry discovery, ingress readiness, workload rollout,
the local image reference, Service and Ingress existence, and the external
health response.

The command must end with fifteen passed checks and zero failed checks.

::: {.callout-note title="Tutor checkpoint C"}
Send your tutor the complete verification output. For any failed check, include
the related `kubectl describe` output before applying a fix.
:::

## Step 12: Run the controlled incident {#sec-lab-00-step-12}

Inject the approved outage:

```bash
make incident-lab-0
```

The incident scales the health deployment to zero without editing Git-owned
configuration. Observe the state:

```bash
kubectl --context kind-supportops-ai -n supportops-platform \
  get deployment,pods,endpointslices
curl -i -H 'Host: platform.supportops.local' \
  http://127.0.0.1/healthz
```

Expect no ready backend and normally HTTP 503. Explain the evidence chain:

1. ingress accepts the request;
2. the Ingress still points to the Service;
3. the Service has no ready endpoint;
4. the deployment’s observed replica count differs from repository desired state.

Do not edit the YAML to zero replicas. The incident is observed-state drift.

::: {.callout-note title="Tutor checkpoint D"}
Send your tutor the Deployment and EndpointSlice output plus the HTTP status.
State which component is healthy and which boundary has failed.
:::

## Step 13: Recover from desired state {#sec-lab-00-step-13}

Recover by reconciling the committed manifest and rerunning verification:

```bash
make recover-lab-0
```

The target reapplies `platform/foundation/platform-health.yaml`, waits for the
rollout, and executes the full verifier. This is preferable to an undocumented
imperative repair because the repository remains the source of truth.

## Step 14: Validate restart behavior {#sec-lab-00-step-14}

Restart the control-plane node container:

```bash
docker restart supportops-ai-control-plane
```

Wait for the API server and node to recover:

```bash
until kubectl --context kind-supportops-ai wait \
  --for=condition=Ready nodes --all --timeout=30s; do
  sleep 5
done

make verify-lab-0
```

The deployment and ingress configuration survive because Kubernetes desired
state is stored in the Kind node. Registry content survives independently in
`supportops-registry-data`.

## Step 15: Capture operational evidence {#sec-lab-00-step-15}

Capture text evidence after recovery:

```bash
make evidence-lab-0
find evidence/lab-00 -maxdepth 1 -type f -print
```

Review every file. The evidence records versions, nodes, namespaces, workload
state, and the endpoint response. The directory is ignored because it contains
machine-specific observations. Attach it to the pull request or summarize its
content there.

Read the runbook before delivery:

```bash
sed -n '1,240p' docs/lab-architecture/lab-00-runbook.md
```

Confirm that you can distinguish registry, scheduling, workload, Service, and
Ingress failures before declaring the baseline operational.

## Step 16: Prepare the pull request {#sec-lab-00-step-16}

Review source-controlled changes:

```bash
git status --short
git diff --check
git diff
```

Do not add `.cache/`, `.local/`, `_site/`, or `evidence/`. The pull request must
describe:

- the selected resource profile;
- the verified tool and component versions;
- the architecture decision and lifecycle risk;
- the controlled incident and observed HTTP status;
- the recovery mechanism;
- the final `make verify-lab-0` result;
- any workstation-specific limitation discovered during execution.

Commit only after the review is complete:

```bash
git add \
  Makefile .gitattributes .gitignore _quarto.yml \
  index.qmd lab-handbook labs starter-project platform incident-packs verification docs
git commit -m 'Complete Lab 0 trusted local baseline'
```

Push the feature branch and open a pull request using your normal Git workflow.

::: {.callout-note title="Tutor checkpoint E"}
Send your tutor the final verification summary, `git status --short`, and the
draft pull-request description. The tutor should challenge the evidence and
failure reasoning before approving delivery.
:::

## Troubleshooting {#sec-lab-00-troubleshooting}

### Kind is not found

```bash
make install-kind
make doctor
```

Make prepends the repository’s `.local/bin/` directory automatically.

### Docker is unreachable from WSL

Confirm Docker Desktop is running and WSL integration is enabled for the active
distribution. Then run:

```bash
docker context show
docker info
```

### Host ports are already allocated

Identify listeners:

```bash
sudo ss -ltnp | grep -E ':(80|443|5001)[[:space:]]'
```

Stop the conflicting local service. Retain the repository ports so every later
lab uses the same endpoint contract.

### A Pod reports `ImagePullBackOff`

Check the registry and node mapping:

```bash
curl -fsS http://127.0.0.1:5001/v2/
docker ps --filter name=supportops-registry
for node in $(kind get nodes --name supportops-ai); do
  docker exec "$node" \
    cat /etc/containerd/certs.d/localhost:5001/hosts.toml
done
```

Then reconcile the idempotent steps:

```bash
make create-cluster PROFILE="${PROFILE:-16gb}"
make build-platform-health
make deploy-baseline
```

### Ingress returns HTTP 404

Ensure the Host header exactly matches the Ingress rule:

```bash
kubectl --context kind-supportops-ai -n supportops-platform \
  get ingress platform-health -o yaml
curl -i -H 'Host: platform.supportops.local' http://127.0.0.1/
```

### Ingress returns HTTP 503

Inspect ready backend endpoints:

```bash
kubectl --context kind-supportops-ai -n supportops-platform \
  get pods,endpointslices
kubectl --context kind-supportops-ai -n supportops-platform \
  describe deployment platform-health
```

HTTP 503 means ingress has a route but cannot reach a ready backend.

## Cleanup or continuation {#sec-lab-00-cleanup}

Keep the cluster and registry for Lab 1. To rebuild only the cluster while
preserving registry content:

```bash
make destroy-cluster
make bootstrap-lab-0 PROFILE="${PROFILE:-16gb}"
```

To remove the cluster, registry container, and persistent registry volume:

```bash
make destroy-all
```

The second command deletes cached images stored in the local registry volume.
Use it only for an intentional clean reset.

## Platform maturity update {#sec-lab-00-maturity}

The SupportOps AI platform now has a reproducible local Kubernetes foundation,
explicit namespace ownership, a persistent local image boundary, a pinned
ingress controller, a secured health workload, automated verification, and a
tested recovery path.

The platform still lacks durable data and artifact services. Lab 1 builds those
capabilities on this baseline without discarding it.
